text: Hey everyone, my name is Crawley and welcome to module one. Let's be entirely honest. When most people hear the word insurance, their brains immediately picture dense stacks of dry legal paperwork. But insurance isn't just modern red tape. It is an epic centuries old story of human community survival. In this session, we are turning back the clock to explore the history of insurance, uncover core definitions, map out the characteristics of insurable risks, look at contract structures, and break down the six general principles that keep the modern financial world spinning. Let's jump into the time machine. Our first destination is ancient Babylon around 1700 BC under the rule of King Hammurabi. Inside the famous code of Hammurabi, we find the earliest documented roots of a collective community safety net. The laws dictated that if a robber was not caught, the local community held a structural obligation to step in and fully compensate the victim for whatever had been lost.

Furthermore, if a citizen was kidnapped, the community was legally bound to pay exactly one min of silver to their family as a ransom. Centuries later, the structural forerunners of modern commercial insurance shifted to Italy. Maritime merchants needed a smart way to protect dangerous sea voyages, leading to two ancestral credit tools. the bottom rebond which served as a financial security agreement using the physical hull or vessel of the ship as collateral e and respondentia which secured loans using the commercial cargo carried inside the ship. While Europe was developing commercial bonds, rich systems of mutual protection were evolving right here in the Philippines. Long before modern corporations arrived, our communities relied on deep cultural safety nets. First is Paloagan, a highly popular local pooling of money scheme that acts as a homegrown mutual fund. It remains incredibly popular across rural regions where members regularly pay daily, weekly, or monthly contributions to a designated collector.

We also rely on the Bayanihun system, our foundational cultural paradigm of collective community support and shared neighborhood labor. And finally, Abaloy, a dedicated community collection, donation, or help fund that serves as an almsshare or offering to ease a family's financial burden during collective affairs or times of grief. Now, let's head over to London in the year 1689. A man named Edward Lloyd opened a humble coffee shop that quickly became the central meeting place for maritime sailors, merchants, and ship owners. Over hot cups of coffee, these individuals negotiated business deals and informal marine insurance agreements. That tiny coffee shop eventually evolved into Lloyds of London. Today, it is the world's absolute leading market for specialist insurance. But remember this vital detail for your upcoming quiz. Lloyds is not an insurance company. Instead, it functions as a structured association of private individuals who step forward to accept and underwrite risks.

The formal timeline of insurance in the Philippines kicked off in March 1829 when Lloyds of London officially appointed Stretchham Murray and Company as its first local representative. 10 years later in 1839, Russell and Sturgis was designated by the Union Insurance Society of Canton as active agents in Manila. Moving to 1898, Sunlife of Canada formally established its local presence. Then came our major domestic corporate milestones. In 1906, Yakong Llin Fire and Marine Insurance Company was founded as our very first domestic non-life insurance company. And in 1910, Insular Life Insurance Company Limited was established, making history as the first domestic life insurance company.

Today these corporate frameworks are supported by core local organizations like PYRA, PLIA, the Philippine Insurers Club, Philishurers, the Actuarial Society of the Philippines and Sound. All of them work to protect the immense social value of insurance which optimizes our factors of production, stabilizes businesses through indemnification, provides a solid foundation for our credit systems, reduces structural worry, and generates vital capital funds for our national economy. Stick around because in video two we are diving deep into the official playbook of the underwriter.

Welcome back to the ledger. To master insurance operations, you have to speak the exact language of underwriters. Let's start by permanently clearing up the single biggest point of confusion in the entire industry: risk versus peril. Risk is defined strictly as the fundamental uncertainty concerning a loss. It represents the actual subject matter of an insurance policy. Underwriters split risk into two categories. Speculative risk can result in either a gain or a loss, like buying stocks or betting on a game, making it entirely uninsurable. Insurance deals exclusively with pure risk, which can only produce a loss with absolutely zero chance of financial gain. We manage risk through four distinct methods: risk avoidance, loss prevention and reduction, risk retention, or risk transfer via an insurance policy. Now look at peril.

Peril is not the uncertainty itself. It is the actual immediate cause of the loss. The unpredictable event insured against like a fire, an earthquake or a shipwreck. If a peril is the cause of a loss, what makes that loss more likely to happen? That is a hazard. Hazards are factors that actively increase the chance of loss from a peril and underwriters classify them into three distinct buckets. First, we have physical hazards. These are tangible, structural, or environmental dangers that you can physically see or touch, such as stockpiling highly combustible goods inside a building, leaving exposed electrical wiring uncovered, positioning properties in low-lying flood-prone areas, leaving an open manhole on a walkway, or driving a vehicle with defective signal tail lights.

Second are moral hazards. These are internal vulnerabilities directly related to the psychological character, integrity, or honesty of the insured individual. Examples include outright dishonesty, a poor credit standing, or an alarming history of numerous past losses. Third are morale hazards. Notice the spelling difference here. Morale hazards are factors rooted entirely in a person's negative attitude, carelessness, or deep indifference to a loss simply because they know they are fully covered by an insurance policy. This looks like poor property housekeeping or a complete lack of basic loss prevention measures.

Let's talk money and calculations. When an insurance policy is written, the face amount listed on the document is called the total sum insured or TSI. This number represents the absolute maximum limit of the insurer's legal liability. To activate this liability, the insured must pay a consideration value called the premium. The strict general rule of the industry dictates no premium equals no policy and no liability. The only legal exceptions are explicitly documented credit terms or an established structural grace period in life insurance contracts.

When an insurance company totals up its premium production over a specific period of time, that baseline sum is called the gross premiums written or GPW. But taking on massive amounts of risk all alone can cause a company to go under. So insurers protect themselves using reinsurance. Reinsurance is literally defined as an insurance of an insurance. The primary insurer shares a portion of their total sum insured and premiums with another professional reinsurer. When you take the gross premiums written and subtract that outsourced reinsurance data, you are left with the net premiums written or NPW. So who calls the shots behind the scenes? The underwriter is the operational gatekeeper whose duty is to select, evaluate, accept risks, set terms, and determine the exact amount of liability the company will retain.

To operate, every underwriter must be fully licensed by the National Insurance Commission. When policies are sold, they use intermediaries. An agent explicitly represents the insurance company. Non-life agents can represent up to seven companies, while life agents can represent only one. Conversely, an insurance broker represents the client. Brokers must be fully incorporated, registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission, SEC, and require a certified soliciting official. All of these gatekeepers use the law of large numbers to calculate precise loss probabilities. For a risk to even be considered insurable, it must meet five strict structural criteria:

A large number of homogeneous exposure units, minimal catastrophic or simultaneous loss probability, a highly definite and measurable event, an economically feasible cost, and a calculable chance of loss. Next up in video three, we are taking this layout straight into the courtroom to look at insurance contracts.

Order in the court! An insurance policy isn't just a basic corporate receipt. It is a formal contract defined under article 305 of the new civil code as a meeting of minds where one party binds himself to give something or render a service. To be legally enforcable, an agreement must stand on four essential elements. One, agreement consisting of a clear offer by one party and an explicit acceptance by the other. Two, competent parties. The insurer must hold a valid license to do business and the insured cannot be a minor, mentally incompetent, or a defined public enemy. Three, legal purpose. The contract cannot run contrary to law, morals, or public interest.

Four, consideration. the premium payment value exchanged for the assumption of loss. If an agreement violates these pillars, it drops into one of four defective categories. Reissible contracts are technically valid but can be legally set aside because they cause distinct economic damage or involve concealment or misrepresentation. Voidable contracts appear valid on the surface but hold a structural legal flaw like an agreement signed by a minor. Uninforcable contracts occur when an individual executes an agreement completely lacking the legal authority to do so. Void or inexistent contracts have a purpose completely contrary to law, morals, or public policy, like a contract protecting an illegal drug supply. They have zero lawful objective and are void from day one.

Section two of the new insurance code formally defines our subject as an agreement to indemnify another against loss, damage, or liability arising from an unknown or contingent event. When you open up a physical insurance policy, you will find six standard highly structured components. First is the preamble, which states the core purpose of the contract, identifies the two parties, and declares that the premium must be fully paid. Second is the insuring clause also known as the operative clause which outlines the insurer's formal agreement to pay if the loss occurs during the period of insurance due to a covered peril. Third is the attestation clause which officially confirms that the insurer has authenticated the policy by signature.

Fourth is the policy schedule which logs all user specific metrics like your name, address, terms, premium rates and the description or location of the risk. Fifth are the conditions defining the strict operational rights, exclusions and claim duties of both parties. And sixth are endorsements which are separate attached documents used to modify the base policy's original terms, conditions, or scope. Oh, and keep an eye out for a cover note. These are crucial temporary contracts issued to provide coverage for a maximum period of 60 days pending the formal issuance of the final policy document. When a legal dispute over text language arises, courts use strict rules of construction.

Phrasiology must be interpreted based on its plain, ordinary, and popular sense, though strict technical meanings are applied to technical terms. If a clause is genuinely ambiguous, it is always construed strictly against the insurer and liberally in favor of the insured because the client had zero say in writing the text. And if the preprinted terms contradict a handwritten or typed edition, the written portion always wins because it captures the true immediate intention of the contract. This is because insurance contracts possess unique structural qualities. They are aliatory meaning the outcome hinges entirely on chance or an uncertain event. They are contracts of adhesion because the insured must accept the text as written by the company.

They are personal meaning the contract is tied explicitly to the specific character and credit of the owner. If ownership of the property changes, the insurance does not automatically pass to the new owner. They are unilateral, meaning only the insurer makes a legally enforcable promise to pay for losses after the premium is received. And they are conditional because the insured must satisfy specific requirements before collecting a dime. Finally, let's step up to the bench and look at the six general principles of insurance that govern every single claim evaluation. Principle one is utmost good faith. Both parties must completely disclose all material facts that would influence risk decisions or premium pricing.

Breaking this via concealment, neglecting to communicate a material fact or misrepresentation where facts failed to match assertions renders the contract resessible. Principle two is insurable interest. You must have a legal relation to the subject matter such that you suffer financially if it is damaged and benefit from its safety. For property insurance, this interest must exist when the insurance takes effect and when the loss occurs. For life insurance, it must exist only when the insurance takes effect. Principle three is indemnity. This ensures an insured is compensated for actual losses sustained and placed back into the exact same financial position occupied right before the disaster.

You are never allowed to profit from insurance. Life and personal accident insurance are the explicit exceptions here. Indemnity can be delivered via monetary payment, reinstatement, repair min or article replacement. Principle four is subregation. A direct corlary to indemnity. It allows the insurer to stand in the shoes of the insured and pursue legal remedies or third-party recoveries against a negligent party who caused the accident, but only up to the exact amount paid out on the claim. This prevents the insured from recovering twice and profiting. Principle five is contribution. This applies when there is double insurance, meaning multiple policies cover the same interest, matter, and peril.

Insurers pay proportionately to the loss. If a policy carries a standard contribution clause, the insured losses the power to arbitrarily pick one company to pay the whole amount. All insurers pay their ratable proportion. Principle six is proximate cause. This is the active efficient cause that sets a seamless chain of events in motion, bringing about a loss without the intervention of any force started from a new independent source. Proximity is judged entirely by its operational effectiveness, not by which event was closest in time to the accident. All of these parameters are governed by Republic Act number 10,67, otherwise known as the Insurance Code of 2013, which gives the insurance commissioner full authority to regulate companies, agents, and brokers alike.

That wraps up our masterclass text transcript for module 1. You are now fully armed with the knowledge. Scroll directly down to interact with our FAQ component and challenge the interactive module quiz widget to lock in your score. See you in the next module.

Welcome to Module 2! I'm Crawley, your AI learning assistant. Today, we're pivoting away from history and diving straight into the heavy-duty world of engineering insurance. This sector actually kicked off in the 18th century during Great Britain's Industrial Revolution back when the factory system became established and early steam plants were constantly exploding. It got so chaotic that it led to the creation of the very first boiler insurance company, the Steam Boiler Insurance Company in 1858. Today we classify engineering insurance into two strictly distinct operational buckets: non-renewable classes and renewable classes.

Think of non-renewable classes as the under-construction phase. If I am currently building a new factory, a non-renewable policy protects the building structures and the machinery while they are being built or installed. The coverage kicks off the exact moment my construction materials are delivered to the site, and it completely terminates the second the installation, testing, and commissioning are finished and the plant is ready for normal use. On the flip side, renewable classes step in once I turn on the power switch. These are annual policies that cover completed industrial plants and installations after they've been accepted by the owner. By understanding whether a risk is a temporary construction project or a permanent running facility, underwriters can correctly structure the policy. Now that we've established these two core classifications, let's hit the next chapter to look at what happens when things get heavy on the construction site.

All right, Crawley here again. Let's talk about the heavy construction phase. If we are building a massive infrastructure asset, we have to know the difference between CAR and EAR. CAR stands for Contractors All Risk insurance. This policy primarily covers civil works. Think concrete, steel, and physical structures like constructing hospitals, roads, bridges, or warehouse complexes. For CAR, my coverage starts the moment the construction materials are unloaded at the site, and it expires the day the building is taken over or put into service. But what if the project isn't a concrete warehouse shell? What if I am installing the actual machinery inside, like automated conveyor systems, power generators, manufacturing lines, or steam boilers?

That requires EAR, Erection All Risk insurance. EAR shifts the primary focus away from the concrete and onto the electromechanical works. And here is the crucial distinction you'll need for the upcoming quiz. EAR automatically covers any minor civil work components needed to install those machines. More importantly, EAR natively provides cover for the high-risk testing and commissioning phase for brand new machinery to ensure they don't explode when first plugged in. A CAR policy can cover electromechanical testing, too, but only if I explicitly pay extra to add it via a special endorsement. Both policies use a broad insuring proviso that protects against sudden and unforeseen physical losses that aren't specifically excluded, like an accidental fire, a landslide, or a worker accidentally dropping a heavy engine component during assembly. Just remember, CAR is for the structural shell, and EAR is for the operational mechanical muscle. Next up, we look at what happens when the building is finished and filled with sensitive silicon.

Welcome back! Crawley here for our third and final lesson in this module. Once a project is fully built, the keys are handed over, and the facility is operational, you face an entirely new set of risks. Inside a modern corporate office or control room sits millions of pesos worth of server arrays, computerized relay equipment, and communication facilities managing the entire business network. Standard property insurance won't cut it for these highly sensitive systems. We need EI, electronic equipment insurance. EI is an annual renewable all risks policy that covers material damage from short circuits, voltage surges, and lightning all the way to operator lack of skill or gross negligence.

But here is the real kicker that makes EI an absolute lifesaver for a business: it doesn't just buy a replacement computer frame under Section 1: Material Damage. Section 2 covers External Data Media. If a power surge fries the hard drives, Section 2 doesn't just replace the physical discs, it pays for the actual cost of hiring data engineers and spending extra manpower to fully restore the lost information. And Section 3 covers the Increased Cost of Working if a core server breaks down. Section 3 covers the emergency rental fees to hire substitute computer equipment so operations stay completely online and running while repairs are being made. With Section 1 protecting the hardware, Section 2 saving the data, and Section 3 keeping the business online, EI provides a complete safety net for digital operations. Lock in these three sections and you're fully prepared to tackle the comprehensive module quiz next. I'll see you there.

Hey, nice to meet you. I'm Crawl, and I'll be your instructor for today. Every time you get behind the wheel, you hold lives in your hands. Driving is a massive responsibility, and accidents carry a heavy physical and financial toll. That is why motor insurance is not just a requirement, it is a shield. Motor insurance covers the ownership, maintenance, and use of a motor vehicle. In the Philippines, the motor car policy is standard. The rules protect you equally whether you drive a private car, a commercial delivery van, a motorcycle, or a public utility vehicle for hire. Your policy provides two main lifelines. First, physical damage insurance protects your vehicle. Second, liability insurance covers the devastating bodily injuries or property damage your car might cause to others.

The foundation of this protection is Section 1, compulsory third-party liability, or CTPL. You cannot register a car without it. It pays up to 100,000 pesos to help a third party in the event of death or bodily injury. To be clear, a third party means the public or employees. For the coverage to apply, the person driving must be an authorized driver with a valid license. If you have a private car or motorcycle, a driving other car clause even extends this protection when you are driving a vehicle you don't own. But what happens in the chaotic aftermath of a crash? When tragedy strikes, families should not have to endure a lengthy court battle just to pay for emergency hospital care or burial expenses. That is why Section 2, no-fault indemnity exists. It provides immediate compassionate relief up to 15,000 pesos for injury or death without the need to prove who was at fault. To release these funds, the family simply provides basic proof like a police report, medical receipts, or a death certificate. While sections 1 and 2 focus on the human cost, the physical wreckage left behind brings its own stress. Let's turn the page and look at how we pick up the pieces.

Crawl back with you for chapter 2. There is a unique kind of dread when you see your car crushed. To even prepare for this moment, your insurer needs your car's exact details upfront: the year, make, model, and its estimated present value. Under section three, your policy covers loss or damage. This covers collisions, fires, overturning, theft, and malicious acts. If your car is immobilized, the policy even covers the towing fee to the nearest repair shop. However, it does not cover everything. You cannot claim everyday wear and tear, mechanical breakdowns, damage limited strictly to your tires, or malicious damage caused by your own family members. When it is time for repairs, insurers use an authorized repair limit, meaning costs are capped at the manufacturer's price catalog.

You will also share the burden of the repair costs first through depreciation. Because a car loses value over time, if your older car needs brand new replacement parts, you will pay a percentage of that parts cost. Second, you pay a deductible. This is a set minimum amount you pay out of pocket, which keeps the system free of tiny nuisance claims so premiums stay affordable for everyone. But what if the crash is catastrophic? If the damage you cause ruins another person's property or leaves them with life-altering injuries, the basic limits will not be enough. Section four, excess liability insurance acts as your ultimate safety net, paying the massive sums that go beyond the limits of the first two sections. Now that we understand what is covered, we need to talk about the rules that hold this entire system together.

All right, Crawl here again for our final chapter. Insurance is a promise and promises require trust. To even buy a policy, you must have an insurable interest. This means you legally own or have a stake in the car, and you would suffer a true financial loss if it were destroyed. You must also practice utmost good faith. You are required to fully and honestly disclose how you use the vehicle. If you hide the truth, the promise is broken. When a crash happens, the principle of proximate cause dictates that the insurer only pays if a covered peril was the direct active cause of the damage. And through the principle of indemnity, the payout is designed to put you back in the exact financial position you were in right before the accident. No better, no worse.

If you have multiple policies covering the same car, the principle of contribution ensures the companies share the claim. And if another reckless driver hits you, the principle of subrogation allows your insurer to pay your claim immediately, then take over your legal right to pursue the at-fault driver for the money. There are also hard boundaries. You are not protected if you drive outside the Philippines, use the car for racing, or haul highly inflammable materials. Standard policies also will not cover acts of nature like floods or earthquakes unless you specifically add that coverage. Finally, know your rights. You can cancel your policy anytime, though a short period rate scale penalty applies to cover administrative costs. If you and the insurer disagree on a claim amount, arbitration is required before any lawsuits can be filed. Ultimately, your greatest duty is to safeguard your vehicle and drive carefully. Protect yourself, protect others, and respect the road. Thank you for learning with me.
